incident.name: Garden Grove MMA Tank Leak
incident.status_headline: GKN Aerospace methyl methacrylate tank in Garden Grove has developed a visible crack with internal temperature confirmed above 100Â°F; OCFA conducted an all-night mission May 24â€“25 to verify pressure relief and determine whether BLEVE risk is eliminated; 50,000 residents remain under mandatory evacuation order as of May 25, 2026.

evacuation.boundary_text: South of Ball Road, north of Trask Avenue, east of Valley View Street, west of Dale Street (includes portions of Garden Grove, Anaheim, Cypress, Stanton, Buena Park, and Westminster)

official_statements.text: Interim Chief TJ McGovern: 'Firefighters did a recon mission on the tank late Saturday night, during which they discovered a potential crack which could be relieving some internal pressure,' putting relief efforts on a 'new trajectory.'

Interim Chief TJ McGovern: 'The BLEVE threat is the worst-case catastrophic event that we have been talking about. We are not there yet. We need to run this operation tonight.' OCFA announced an all-night mission to confirm whether tank pressure has been released and the explosion threat eliminated.

Captain Brian Yau: 'We know that it's over 100 degrees inside... being near that tank is high risk in itself, because it still has the possibility to have a catastrophic failure.' Officials noted the gauge does not read above 100 so the exact temperature is unknown.

Governor Gavin Newsom proclaimed a state of emergency in Orange County, directing Cal OES and all state agencies to support the county and unlocking additional emergency response resources, including state-owned properties as evacuation shelters.

DA Todd Spitzer announced a probe into GKN Aerospace: 'We are not getting satisfactory answers,' and called on whistleblowers at the company to come forward with information about the tank's condition.

Incident Commander Craig Covey: 'There are literally two options left remaining: One, the tank fails and spills a total of about 6,000 to 7,000 gallons of very bad chemicals into the parking lot and that area. Or two, the tank goes into a thermal runaway and blows up, affecting the tanks that are around it that have fuel or chemicals in them as well.'
